core_intuitions: Matrix multiplication is one computation with three equivalent readings; the outer-product reading expresses C = AB as a sum of rank-1 matrices, which is the conceptual bridge from matmul to low-rank structure.
The SVD factors ANY matrix into rotate-scale-rotate (U, Sigma, V^T): the orthogonal factors U and V preserve lengths and angles, and ALL the stretching lives in Sigma.

A fast-decaying singular-value spectrum means keeping only the top-r rank-1 terms is the BEST rank-r approximation (Eckart-Young), and it captures almost all the signal while the tail is noise — this is the theoretical heart of the chapter and directly motivates LoRA.

Backprop through a linear map is the transpose rule: dL/dW = X^T G and dL/dX = G W^T. The forward matmul's transposes reappear in the backward pass; this is the single most important identity in deep learning.

LoRA works because fine-tuning updates are low-rank: replacing Delta-W with a product B*A squeezes activations through a width-r bottleneck, cutting trainable parameters from d^2 to 2dr with minimal quality loss.

new_figures.name: matmul-three-views
new_figures.concept: One product C = AB read three ways: dot products, column combinations, and a sum of rank-1 outer products.
new_figures.rationale: Almost everyone knows matmul only as 'rows times columns'. The chapter explicitly flags the outer-product / rank-1 reading as 'surprisingly important' because it is the bridge from ordinary matmul to SVD and low-rank approximation. Seeing the SAME small product sliced three ways builds the reframe that makes the later SVD and LoRA sections click. This is foundational, not decorative: every later figure in the chapter depends on this mental model.
new_figures.placement: Matrix Multiplication: The Workhorse Operation -> after the 'Three equivalent views' subsection
new_figures.brief: A wide static infographic (viewBox ~ 820x360) with three clearly separated panels sharing ONE tiny running example: A is 3x2, B is 2x4, so C = AB is 3x4. Use small integer-free grids (just cells, no invented numbers) rendered as v-fill rectangles with v-grid interior lines; label matrices with their letter (v-accent) and dims (e.g. '3x2', '2x4', '3x4') in muted mono. Panel titles in bold serif/sans. PANEL 1 'View 1 - Dot products': draw A, B, and C side by side; highlight ONE row of A (row i, accent stroke) and ONE column of B (col j, accent stroke), draw two faint guide lines converging onto a single highlighted cell C_ij; caption underneath: 'C_ij = row_i(A) . col_j(B)'. PANEL 2 'View 2 - Column combinations': draw B with its j-th column highlighted, an arrow 'A x' to C with its j-th column highlighted; small annotation showing C's j-th column as a weighted stack/blend of A's TWO columns (two faint tinted column swatches with '+' between them), caption: 'each output column = a blend of A’s columns'. PANEL 3 'View 3 - Outer products (rank-1 sum)': show C = (col_1 of A)(row_1 of B) + (col_2 of A)(row_2 of B); render each term as a thin tall column swatch times a thin wide row swatch forming a full 3x4 tile, with a '+' between the two rank-1 tiles and '=' to C; label each term 'rank-1' in muted text. Add a short bridging annotation beneath panel 3 (accent, small): 'truncate this sum -> low-rank approximation (see SVD)'. Theme-safe: all fills/strokes via v-* classes or var(); no hardcoded primary colors; everything inside the viewBox with generous gutters so nothing clips; clear left-to-right, top reading order 1->2->3.

new_figures.name: svd-geometry-and-lowrank
new_figures.concept: SVD as rotate-scale-rotate, plus the singular-value spectrum cliff and Eckart-Young top-r truncation.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's thesis and the payoff of the whole chapter: it unifies 'what SVD IS' (the Key-Takeaway rotate-scale-rotate) with 'what SVD is FOR' (best low-rank approximation), and it is the direct rationale for LoRA. The rapid singular-value decay is an empirical claim the reader must SEE to believe, and a static frame captures it cleanly. Without this figure the LoRA preview reads as an unmotivated trick.
new_figures.placement: Eigendecomposition and the Singular Value Decomposition -> at the 'Best low-rank approximation: the Eckart-Young theorem' subsection
new_figures.brief: A static two-panel infographic (viewBox ~ 820x360), panels visually separated by a thin v-grid divider. LEFT PANEL 'SVD is rotate - scale - rotate': show the geometric action on a 2-D shape in four small stages left-to-right connected by arrows. Stage 0: a unit circle with two orthonormal axis arrows. Stage 1: apply V^T -> the SAME circle, axes rotated (label arrow 'Vᵀ rotate'). Stage 2: apply Sigma -> the circle becomes an ELLIPSE stretched along the axes, long semi-axis labeled 'sigma_1', short labeled 'sigma_2' (label arrow 'Sigma scale'). Stage 3: apply U -> the ellipse rotated to its final orientation (label arrow 'U rotate'). Small caption under the panel: 'U, V preserve lengths & angles; ALL stretching lives in Sigma.' Keep the circle/ellipse small and clean; use v-accent for the ellipse outline and v-muted for axis arrows. RIGHT PANEL 'Best rank-r approximation (Eckart-Young)': (a) a compact singular-value BAR CHART: ~10 vertical bars, the first 4-5 tall and clearly decreasing, then a sharp CLIFF to a long flat 'noise floor' of tiny bars; a dashed vertical 'keep top-r' cut line after the tall bars, region left of the cut lightly tinted v-fill labeled 'signal', region right labeled 'noise'; axis label 'sigma_i' with bars unlabeled by number (illustrative only, invent no values). (b) beneath the chart, the truncated sum shown iconically: 'A_r = sigma_1 u_1 v_1ᵀ + sigma_2 u_2 v_2ᵀ + ...' as a few shrinking rank-1 tiles, with faded ghost tiles after the cut. (c) small accent annotation: 'error = sqrt( sum_{i>r} sigma_i^2 )' and a one-line bridge 'fast-decaying spectrum => LoRA works'. Theme-safe v-* classes / var() only; no invented numbers on axes; ensure the geometry stages and the bar chart do not overlap and all labels sit fully inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: linear-layer-backprop-transpose
new_figures.concept: Forward Y = XW; backward reuses the transposes: dL/dW = X^T G and dL/dX = G W^T.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter names these two identities 'the single most important' in backprop and 'the most important formula to have on the tip of your tongue', and it is an explicit Interview Corner. The intuition that the FORWARD matmul's operands come back TRANSPOSED in the backward pass, and that shapes must match, is exactly the kind of thing a diagram cements far better than prose. Foundational for every later networks/autodiff chapter.
new_figures.placement: Matrix Calculus for Backpropagation -> at the 'Gradient of a linear layer' subsection
new_figures.brief: A static infographic (viewBox ~ 820x340) with two horizontal lanes and a mirror symmetry between them. TOP LANE 'Forward' (arrows flow LEFT -> RIGHT): three tiles X (labeled 'B x D_in'), an operator 'x' , W ('D_in x D_out'), then '=' , then Y ('B x D_out'). Tiles are v-fill rects with v-accent letters and muted dim labels; a bold arrow along the top labeled 'forward: Y = X W'. BOTTOM LANE 'Backward' (arrows flow RIGHT -> LEFT): the upstream gradient G ('B x D_out', same shape as Y, drawn directly beneath Y and tinted accent) ENTERS from the right and FORKS into two products drawn as small matmul groupings: (1) 'Xᵀ . G' producing dL/dW ('D_in x D_out', drawn beneath W, same footprint as W) and (2) 'G . Wᵀ' producing dL/dX ('B x D_in', drawn beneath X, same footprint as X). Draw faint vertical alignment guides so the reader sees dL/dW sits under W and dL/dX sits under X (same shapes). Show the transpose visually: render Xᵀ and Wᵀ as the forward tiles flipped/mirrored with a small 'ᵀ' badge, to make 'the backward pass reuses the transposed operand' literal. Central caption banner between the lanes (accent): 'backward = multiply by the transpose'. Small shape-check note: 'shapes must match the thing you’re differentiating'. Theme-safe v-* classes only; ensure the two lanes are clearly separated and the fork arrows do not cross-clutter; everything inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: lora-lowrank-bottleneck
new_figures.concept: y = W0 x + B(A x): a frozen full-rank W0 plus a trainable rank-r path that squeezes activations through a width-r waist; params drop from d^2 to 2dr.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter builds LoRA with code and dimensions and it is the recurring application thread of the book. This figure turns the algebra W = W0 + BA into a data-flow intuition — the 'information bottleneck' of rank r and the frozen-vs-trainable split — that a reader carries into the entire post-training section. It is the concrete payoff of the SVD/low-rank thesis, distinct from the SVD figure (that one is about approximation; this one is about the forward path and parameter budget).
new_figures.placement: Low-Rank Approximations and the LoRA Preview -> at the 'LoRA in matrix algebra terms' subsection
new_figures.brief: A static data-flow infographic (viewBox ~ 820x340). Input vector x on the left splits into TWO parallel paths that recombine at a sum node on the right. TOP (frozen) PATH: a large square tile W0 labeled 'W0  (d x d)' drawn with a small lock glyph and muted/greyed fill and a 'frozen - no gradient' tag; arrow x -> W0 -> gives 'W0 x'. BOTTOM (trainable) PATH: x -> a WIDE-to-NARROW trapezoid/tile A ('r x d', maps d -> r) that visibly narrows to a thin vertical WAIST of height r (label the waist prominently 'rank r bottleneck', r << d), then a NARROW-to-WIDE tile B ('d x r', maps r -> d) that widens back; use v-accent for A and B to signal 'trainable'. Emphasize the waist as the single narrow channel all adaptation must pass through. Both paths meet at a circled '+' (sum) producing y, with the formula shown above: 'y = W0 x + B (A x)'. Add an init note near B (small, muted): 'B = 0 at init  =>  Delta-W = 0  (start from pretrained)'. Bottom-right: a simple two-bar PARAMETER comparison, one tall bar 'full fine-tune: d^2' vs one tiny bar 'LoRA: 2 d r', illustrative proportions only, NO invented numbers on the bars. Theme-safe: frozen path muted/v-grid tones, trainable path v-accent; all via v-* classes / var(); ensure the bottleneck waist, tiles, sum node, and param bars are all legible and inside the viewBox with no overlap.

summary: This foundational linear-algebra chapter currently has ZERO figures despite being unusually concept-dense, so it is figure-poor and concept-rich. I propose four disciplined STATIC infographics that each serve a stated core intuition and build a single connected thread: (1) matmul's three views, ending on the outer-product/rank-1 reading that bridges to low rank; (2) the SVD as rotate-scale-rotate plus the singular-value cliff and Eckart-Young top-r truncation — the chapter's thesis and the rationale for everything downstream; (3) the linear-layer backprop transpose rule, which the text calls the single most important identity in deep learning; and (4) the LoRA rank-r bottleneck data flow, the concrete payoff of the low-rank thesis. All are static because a single well-composed frame conveys each idea; none require animation. Priorities: figures 1-3 are P1 (must-have), figure 4 is P2. Briefs are theme-safe (v-* classes only), use small illustrative shapes with no invented numbers, and keep all content inside the viewBox.
